items.quote: Cadence leadership meeting this week to listen to final budget requests.
Decision pushed out until mid-July. Down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad.

metrics.narrative: Cadence articulated substantial qualitative pain but never quantified it. Devesh Chadha described wanting 'one platform to manage both buy-side vendor contracts and sell-side legal/sales agreements' (Intro Call, 23 Jan 2026), and named a 500,000-document legacy migration burden with 'we need to understand the impact of that from a cost perspective' (Peter Hughes, Cadence/Zycus Sync, 13 Apr 2026). Renewal metrics today are tracked manually by the sourcing team (Discovery, 25 Feb 2026). No cost, cycle-time, or ROI figure was ever stated by the buyer on any call read; quantifying the migration/renewal savings case remains the open move.

champion.narrative: Peter Hughes is the closest thing to a champion: he is named the internal 'CLM project lead' (Next Step, 26 May 2026), coordinated every workshop and scheduling decision from January through April, and was the sole recipient of the 9 Jun 2026 Zycus rep-transition note, making him the primary conduit into the buying committee. However, no call shows him explicitly advocating for Zycus internally or pushing back on Ironclad; his role reads as neutral process-owner rather than active promoter, and there has been no direct contact evidenced since the transition note. This is 'developing, at risk': developed = sole steady point of contact and internal project ownership; missing = demonstrated internal advocacy and any contact since early June.

competition.narrative: Cadence's legal team stated on the very first call that it was 'impressed by tools like iClad ' and has pressing AI requirements (Devesh Chadha, Intro Call, 23 Jan 2026) — a preference never directly countered since. Coupa is the incumbent CLM tool being displaced ('Currently using Coupa for CLM, seeking one platform...', 23 Jan 2026), and two low-cost intake tools ('Tonkin' and 'Yep') were evaluated and rejected for insufficient capability relative to cost (23 Jan 2026). As of 9 Jun 2026, Cadence is 'down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad' — Zycus is a live finalist, but the legal constituency's early-stated preference for the rival is the wedge Ironclad still holds.

identify_pain.narrative: Confirmed pain: disparate contract intake via Salesforce and manual email, 'lacking standardized orchestration' (Discovery, 25 Feb 2026); renewal metrics tracked manually by the sourcing team causing inefficiency (Discovery, 25 Feb 2026); and a 500,000-document legacy migration with real cost/cleanliness uncertainty ('what's the cost to us to do that... so that what goes into the solution is relevant, right, and not garbage' — Peter Hughes, Cadence/Zycus Sync, 13 Apr 2026). Sales-finance also raised an adoption-burden concern about extra process overhead (CLM Workshop Follow-Up, 21 Apr 2026). Pain owner: Peter Hughes (project lead) and Melanie Hamm (contracts/legal side).

sources: Avoma System Architecture and Integration 6 Apr 2026
paper_process.narrative: Only one paper-process step has occurred: the Zycus MSA was submitted to Cadence legal on 21 Apr 2026 (Next Step, completed) to 'kick off parallel legal review and avoid bottlenecks at procurement stage' (CLM Workshop Follow-Up, 21 Apr 2026). No redlines, SOW discussion, or InfoSec sign-off have been evidenced since; SOC 1/SOC 2 documents were shared during the 6 Apr 2026 architecture review but no closure was confirmed. With the close date 2 days out and a vendor decision not expected until mid-July, contracting has not meaningfully started; confirming legal's actual review status is the visibility gap.

economic_buyer.narrative: No named Cadence executive with budget sign-off authority has appeared on any of the 13 read calls across the full cycle (23 Jan 2026 to 28 Apr 2026). Next Step entries reference an unnamed 'Cadence leadership' group that reviews final budget requests (26 May 2026: 'Cadence leadership meeting this week to listen to final budget requests') and controls the mid-July vendor decision (9 Jun 2026), but this person/group has never had direct Zycus contact. Devesh Chadha (Director, Procurement COE) is the closest proxy and is the named Decision Maker on the Salesforce contact role, but budget approval sits above him. Getting a direct introduction to the actual budget approver is the single highest-priority gap-closing move.

decision_process.narrative: The call sequence itself maps the process: intro (23 Jan) → discovery (25 Feb) → intake/Merlin demos and IT architecture review (26 Mar-6 Apr) → functional workshops split by user group, legal/procurement/business (6-28 Apr) → sandbox POC (proposed 2 days, buyer pushed for 5) → down-select to a final two by 9 Jun. Approval runs through 'Cadence leadership' budget review (26 May) feeding a vendor decision now pushed to mid-July. Jason Lieu explicitly flagged running a parallel sandbox with the other finalist (Ironclad) on more open terms (28 Apr 2026: 'we're running a similar sandbox with the other candidate solution, and we don't have the same constraints'), confirming a structured, competitive bake-off process.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are extensively documented across calls: a single CLM to serve procurement, legal, and sales (Devesh Chadha, Intro Call 23 Jan 2026); demo coverage of intake orchestration via the relevant stakeholder, clause libraries, AI-driven redlining, Word plug-in, and SAP/DocuSign integration (Discovery, 25 Feb 2026); and detailed functional requirements from Melanie Hamm covering coded templates, conditional clause logic, and sales-finance access control (CLM Use Cases session, 20 Apr 2026). Top success metrics named by the buyer: 'a seamless end-to-end solution serving procurement, legal, and sales, efficient transaction throughput, AI-driven agreement creation, and strict infosec compliance' (Discovery, 25 Feb 2026).

Coupa is the incumbent CLM tool being actively displaced, not a competitive threat for the new award. Two low-cost intake tools ('Tonkin', 'Yep') were evaluated and rejected on cost/capability grounds in January 2026 and remain dormant.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A deeply evaluated, multi-thread deal (13 calls read across legal, procurement, IT and sourcing) that cleared technical and workshop gates and reached the final two, but is undermined by a never-engaged economic buyer, a rival with an early stated legal preference, a stalled paper process, and a close date that no longer matches the buyer's own timeline.

deal_risk.text: The 10 Jul 2026 close date is not defensible: the buyer's own 9 Jun 2026 update pointed to a mid-July decision, and no vendor decision, contracting, or signature activity has occurred.
The absence of any economic-buyer engagement, six months into the cycle, is the single largest gate on this deal regardless of how advanced the technical evaluation is.
Ironclad remains an active, named finalist with legal's early stated preference for it never overturned in any later call.

win_position.text: Six months of deep, multi-thread evaluation (legal, procurement, IT, sourcing) culminated in Zycus being confirmed as one of two finalists as of the 9 Jun 2026 update.

The buyer ran an extended sandbox/POC, requested and received a second customer reference, and produced unusually detailed functional requirements, signaling real evaluation investment rather than a box-checking RFP.
No economic buyer has appeared on a single Zycus call in the full evaluation history; the unnamed Cadence leadership approver has never been engaged directly.
Cadence's legal team named a rival tool as impressive on the very first call (23 Jan 2026), a preference that has never been directly countered with Zycus-specific proof points.

deal_momentum.text: A genuine buyer-side email reply was received 19 Jun 2026 (20 days ago), and the new deal owner has since been introduced to the procurement director and is circulating an internal roadmap and event invitation as recently as 29 Jun and 2 Jul 2026 (7 days ago).

The formal vendor decision, expected 'first week of June' per the 26 May 2026 note, slipped once already to 'mid-July' by 9 Jun 2026, with no confirmation the leadership budget meeting referenced 26 May 2026 ever concluded.
No contracting activity (redlines, SOW, signature) has surfaced since the MSA was sent to Cadence legal on 21 Apr 2026, over ten weeks with no visible legal-track progress.
A rep-ownership transition occurred around 9 Jun 2026, introducing ramp-up risk for the new owner at a decision-critical moment.

customer_commitment.text: The buyer requested an extended sandbox (2 to 5 business days) and mobilized roughly 15 users across legal, procurement, IT and sales-finance to test the platform hands-on.
A second customer reference call was explicitly requested and completed by 28 Apr 2026, a typically late-funnel buying signal.
iRisk was pulled out of the CLM commercial bundle at the buyer's own request on 7 Apr 2026, shrinking deal scope and signaling some spend caution.

hard.ais_why:
• The 95-minute 4/3/2026 workshop generated 22 action items. Devika Sanil's commitment to send Jason Lieu and the team the list of external data sources behind the AI risk model is a buyer-pulled ask, and Kevin Cipollaro's cost breakdown is split into CLM, Intake, and iRisk line items, confirming Merlin Intake and AI-driven iRisk are inside the priced scope.
• Other buyer-pulled commitments include the SAP adapter intro to Devesh Chadha, sub-tier mapping plus FedRAMP authorization status, the product roadmap going to Jason and Devesh, and a standard-versus-premium support table. Cadence is driving the agenda. The earlier 1/25/2026 rep note that Cadence is unimpressed with Coupa's Agentic AI is the competitive opening Zycus is now stepping into.
• POC kickoff is scheduled for 5/12/2026, the MSA went in on 4/21, two references provided, and the project lead has told the team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor. Transcript-level keyword review confirmed AI and Merlin signal present; champion, POC scope including Intake and iRisk, and budget alignment justify High confidence.
